import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        rest_capacity:   remaining vehicle capacity
        demands:         demand of every node (index 0 = depot demand = 0)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute distances from current node to all feasible candidates
    dist_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Primary criterion: nearest distance
    min_dist = np.min(dist_from_current)
    nearest_mask = dist_from_current <= min_dist + 1e-9
    candidates = unvisited_nodes[nearest_mask]
    
    # If multiple nearest nodes, apply savings-based tie‑breaker:
    # maximize s_ij = d_i0 + d_0j - d_ij  (here i=current_node, j=candidate)
    # This prefers nodes that lie on the line between depot and current position.
    if len(candidates) > 1:
        savings = (distance_matrix[current_node][depot] +
                   distance_matrix[depot][candidates] -
                   distance_matrix[current_node][candidates])
        best_idx = np.argmax(savings)
        return candidates[best_idx]
    
    return candidates[0]

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        rest_capacity:   remaining vehicle capacity
        demands:         demand of every node (index 0 = depot demand = 0)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute distances from current node to all unvisited feasible nodes
    dists = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Find the minimum distance among feasible nodes
    min_dist = np.min(dists)
    
    # Identify all nodes achieving this minimum distance
    candidates_idx = np.where(dists == min_dist)[0]
    candidates = unvisited_nodes[candidates_idx]
    
    # If only one candidate, choose it directly
    if len(candidates) == 1:
        chosen = candidates[0]
    else:
        # Tie‑breaker: prefer the candidate farthest from the depot
        # (to delay long detours later)
        depot_dists = distance_matrix[depot, candidates]
        chosen = candidates[np.argmax(depot_dists)]
    
    # Early return check: after serving 'chosen', will we still be able to
    # reach at least one other remaining customer without returning to depot?
    remaining_after = unvisited_nodes[unvisited_nodes != chosen]
    if len(remaining_after) > 0:
        future_feasible_demands = demands[remaining_after] <= (rest_capacity - demands[chosen])
        if not np.any(future_feasible_demands):
            # Check whether going back to depot now is cheaper than going to 'chosen' and then back
            direct_return_saving = (
                distance_matrix[current_node, depot] +
                distance_matrix[depot, chosen] -
                distance_matrix[current_node, chosen]
            )
            # If returning now doesn't increase cost much (< 5% margin), trigger early return
            # This helps compact routing when no good follow‑up exists.
            if direct_return_saving < 0.05 * distance_matrix[current_node, chosen]:
                return depot
    
    return chosen

    min_dist = np.min(dists)
    
    # Identify all nodes achieving this minimum distance
    candidates_idx = np.where(dists == min_dist)[0]
    candidates = unvisited_nodes[candidates_idx]
    
    # If only one candidate, choose it directly
    if len(candidates) == 1:
        chosen = candidates[0]
    else:
        # Tie‑breaker: prefer the candidate farthest from the depot
        # (to delay long detours later)
        depot_dists = distance_matrix[depot, candidates]
        chosen = candidates[np.argmax(depot_dists)]
    
    # Early return check: after serving 'chosen', will we still be able to
    # reach at least one other remaining customer without returning to depot?
    remaining_after = unvisited_nodes[unvisited_nodes != chosen]
    if len(remaining_after) > 0:
        future_feasible_demands = demands[remaining_after] <= (rest_capacity - demands[chosen])
        if not np.any(future_feasible_demands):
            # Check whether going back to depot now is cheaper than going to 'chosen' and then back
            direct_return_saving = (
                distance_matrix[current_node, depot] +
                distance_matrix[depot, chosen] -
                distance_matrix[current_node, chosen]
            )
            # If returning now doesn't increase cost much (< 5% margin), trigger early return
            # This helps compact routing when no good follow‑up exists.
            if direct_return_saving < 0.05 * distance_matrix[current_node, chosen]:
                return depot
    
    return chosen

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction."""
    # If no feasible unvisited customers remain, return to depot
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute distances from current node to all unvisited feasible customers
    dists = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Find the nearest feasible customer
    nearest_idx = np.argmin(dists)
    nearest_customer = unvisited_nodes[nearest_idx]
    
    # Safety check (though unvisited_nodes should already be capacity‑feasible)
    if demands[nearest_customer] <= rest_capacity:
        return nearest_customer
    else:
        # This case should not occur given the problem statement,
        # but fallback to depot if capacity constraint is violated.
        return depot

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute distances from current node to all unvisited candidates
    dist_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Primary criterion: minimal distance to nearest feasible neighbor
    min_dist = np.min(dist_from_current)
    nearest_mask = dist_from_current == min_dist
    nearest_candidates = unvisited_nodes[nearest_mask]
    
    # If multiple nearest at same distance, apply savings tie-breaker
    if len(nearest_candidates) > 1:
        # Savings s(u) = d(depot,current) + d(depot,u) - d(current,u)
        # For fixed current, d(depot,current) is constant, so we maximize
        # [d(depot,u) - d(current,u)] which is equivalent to minimizing
        # [d(current,u) - d(depot,u)]
        savings_scores = distance_matrix[depot, nearest_candidates] - distance_matrix[current_node, nearest_candidates]
        best_idx = np.argmax(savings_scores)
        chosen = nearest_candidates[best_idx]
        
        # Additional angular coherence check if still tied (very rare but ensures determinism)
        # Use deterministic atan2-like proxy: angle rank based on coordinates relative to depot
        # Since we don't have explicit coordinates, we approximate using vector differences
        # But given API constraints, we fall back to smallest index to remain deterministic
        ties_after_savings = nearest_candidates[savings_scores == np.max(savings_scores)]
        if len(ties_after_savings) > 1:
            # Fallback to smallest index for strict determinism
            chosen = np.min(ties_after_savings)
    else:
        chosen = nearest_candidates[0]
    
    # Early return check: only consider returning to depot voluntarily if no feasible customers left
    # Here unvisited_nodes already capacity-feasible, so never force depot unless empty.
    # However, we can add an optional rule: if the nearest candidate's insertion makes future routes worse
    # due to detour, we might close route early. Following strategy cards, we keep simple:
    # Never voluntarily return to depot here because unvisited_nodes is non-empty and feasible.
    # This matches the "or depot only for voluntary return" rule but we don't exercise it.
    return int(chosen)

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute savings‑inspired score: closer nodes preferred, but also favor those that leave more residual capacity relative to their demand?
    # Actually, we keep pure nearest‑neighbor but add an early‑return safety rule:
    # If no unvisited node can be served from the depot without exceeding capacity later, 
    # we might still go back early? Not required here — selection only among already capacity‑feasible nodes.
    
    distances = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    min_idx = np.argmin(distances)
    best_candidate = unvisited_nodes[min_idx]
    
    # Optional tie‑breaker: if multiple have same min distance, pick largest demand to save space? 
    # But specification says just return argmin of distance → that’s what we do.
    return best_candidate

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction."""
    # If no feasible customers remain, return to depot
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot

    # Nearest feasible customer distances from current_node
    dists = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    min_dist = np.min(dists)

    # Tolerance: consider ties when distances differ by less than 1e-9 * min_dist + 1e-12
    tol = max(1e-9 * min_dist, 1e-12)
    near_mask = np.abs(dists - min_dist) <= tol
    near_indices = unvisited_nodes[near_mask]

    # If a single nearest candidate, return it immediately
    if len(near_indices) == 1:
        return near_indices[0]

    # Regret tie-breaking among equally-near candidates
    best_regret = -np.inf
    chosen = near_indices[0]  # fallback

    for cand in near_indices:
        # Simulate insertion of 'cand' now: cost = distance from current_node to cand
        first_cost = distance_matrix[current_node, cand]

        # Second-best feasible insertion point among other unvisited_nodes
        other_unvisited = unvisited_nodes[unvisited_nodes != cand]
        if len(other_unvisited) == 0:
            # No other customers left, regret is zero
            regret = 0.0
        else:
            # For each other customer, imagine inserting it after 'cand' vs directly from current_node?
            # Instead, simpler and correct: regret = extra distance incurred if we postpone this candidate.
            # Compute the increase in cost if we insert this candidate later from the depot instead of now.
            # That is: direct insertion cost = first_cost (from current to cand).
            # Postponed cost = distance(depot, cand) because we would start a new route for it.
            postponed_cost = distance_matrix[depot, cand]
            # But regret definition used here: second-best - best where "best" is first_cost.
            # We interpret "second-best" as the best alternative position in a future route,
            # i.e., the cheapest way to insert it if not taken now, which is starting a fresh route at depot.
            # However, to align with typical regret heuristic, we compute for this candidate:
            # best possible insertion elsewhere (among other feasible locations) minus best insertion now.
            # Since we are at a single current node, the "other positions" are limited to starting a new route.
            # A more robust look-ahead: for each candidate, evaluate all other unvisited as potential previous stops?
            # Given API constraints, we approximate regret as the difference between
            # the nearest neighbor distance from the depot to this candidate and the current distance,
            # penalizing candidates far from the depot when they are equally near from current.
            # Actually, standard regret for construction: for each candidate j,
            # let d1 = min over feasible current insertions (here just one: current_node),
            # let d2 = second minimum over feasible current insertions (if there were alternatives).
            # With only one feasible insertion position (the current vehicle), d2 equals postponing to a new route.
            # So d2 = distance(depot, j).
            # Then regret_j = d2 - d1 = distance(depot, j) - distance(current_node, j).
            d2 = distance_matrix[depot, cand]
            regret_val = d2 - first_cost

        if regret_val > best_regret:
            best_regret = regret_val
            chosen = cand

    return chosen

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        rest_capacity:   remaining vehicle capacity
        demands:         demand of every node (index 0 = depot demand = 0)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute savings-based score: prefer closer nodes and penalize low capacity utilization
    distances = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Find the nearest node
    nearest_idx = np.argmin(distances)
    nearest_node = unvisited_nodes[nearest_idx]
    
    # Dynamic rule: if remaining capacity after serving nearest is less than 
    # half of its demand, consider returning to depot unless there's a very close alternative.
    # Here we use a simple look-ahead: if the nearest node's demand leaves little room (<20% of capacity),
    # check if another node allows better future flexibility while still being reasonably close.
    cap_threshold = 0.2 * (rest_capacity + demands[nearest_node])
    
    if (rest_capacity - demands[nearest_node] < cap_threshold):
        # Try to find a node that uses more of the remaining capacity but isn't too far
        scores = []
        for i, node in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
            dist = distances[i]
            util = demands[node] / rest_capacity
            # Score balances proximity and capacity utilization
            # Normalize by average values (avoid division by zero)
            avg_dist = np.mean(distances) if len(distances) > 1 else 1.0
            norm_dist = dist / max(avg_dist, 1e-6)
            # Prefer higher utilization when capacity is tight, but keep distance reasonable
            # Higher score means worse choice; we want to minimize this composite measure
            penalty = norm_dist * (1.5 - util)  # lower util increases penalty if dist similar
            scores.append(penalty)
        
        best_in_scores = unvisited_nodes[np.argmin(scores)]
        # Only choose this alternative if it's within 50% more distance than nearest
        if distance_matrix[current_node, best_in_scores] <= 1.5 * distance_matrix[current_node, nearest_node]:
            return best_in_scores
        # Otherwise stick with nearest
        return nearest_node
    
    return nearest_node

        # Only choose this alternative if it's within 50% more distance than nearest
        if distance_matrix[current_node, best_in_scores] <= 1.5 * distance_matrix[current_node, nearest_node]:
            return best_in_scores
        # Otherwise stick with nearest
        return nearest_node
    
    return nearest_node

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        rest_capacity:   remaining vehicle capacity
        demands:         demand of every node (index 0 = depot demand = 0)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute savings-based score: prefer closer nodes and penalize low capacity utilization
    distances = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Find the nearest node
    nearest_idx = np.argmin(distances)
    nearest_node = unvisited_nodes[nearest_idx]
    
    # Dynamic rule: if remaining capacity after serving nearest is less than 
    # half of its demand, consider returning to depot unless there's a very close alternative.
    # Here we use a simple look-ahead: if the nearest node's demand leaves little room (<20% of capacity),
    # check if another node allows better future flexibility while still being reasonably close.
    cap_threshold = 0.2 * (rest_capacity + demands[nearest_node])
    
    if (rest_capacity - demands[nearest_node] < cap_threshold):
        # Try to find a node that uses more of the remaining capacity but isn't too far
        scores = []
        for i, node in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
            dist = distances[i]
            util = demands[node] / rest_capacity
            # Score balances proximity and capacity utilization
            # Normalize by average values (avoid division by zero)
            avg_dist = np.mean(distances) if len(distances) > 1 else 1.0
            norm_dist = dist / max(avg_dist, 1e-6)
            # Prefer higher utilization when capacity is tight, but keep distance reasonable
            # Higher score means worse choice; we want to minimize this composite measure
            penalty = norm_dist * (1.5 - util)  # lower util increases penalty if dist similar
            scores.append(penalty)
        
        best_in_scores = unvisited_nodes[np.argmin(scores)]
        # Only choose this alternative if it's within 50% more distance than nearest
        if distance_matrix[current_node, best_in_scores] <= 1.5 * distance_matrix[current_node, nearest_node]:
            return best_in_scores
        # Otherwise stick with nearest
        return nearest_node
    
    return nearest_node

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    # If no feasible customers remain, return to depot
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute savings-based score: distance from current to candidate minus distance from candidate to depot
    # This balances proximity to current location and closeness to depot for future return
    scores = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes] - distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Find the candidate with minimum score (most negative is best: close to current and far from depot is penalized? Wait, correction: 
    # Actually, we want to minimize distance from current, but also consider that going to a node near depot might be good later.
    # Let's use a simple ratio: distance_to_candidate / (distance_candidate_depot + epsilon) to favor nearby nodes that are not extremely close to depot?
    # But standard nearest neighbor already works; we'll enhance with a penalty for high demand relative to capacity.
    # However, the problem states unvisited_nodes are already filtered for capacity.
    # We'll use a composite measure: distance weighted by demand fraction.
    
    distances = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]
    demand_fractions = demands[unvisited_nodes] / rest_capacity
    # Combine: prefer closer and lower demand (lower fraction)
    combined_scores = distances * (1 + 0.3 * demand_fractions)
    
    # Return the node with smallest combined score
    return unvisited_nodes[np.argmin(combined_scores)]

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        rest_capacity:   remaining vehicle capacity
        demands:         demand of every node (index 0 = depot demand = 0)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot

    # Parameter settings: base tolerance factor, minimum absolute tolerance, and candidate count threshold
    alpha = 5e-3          # base fractional tolerance
    beta = 1e-10          # minimum absolute tolerance
    gamma = 3             # number of tied candidates that triggers expansion

    # Step 1: Nearest feasible selection
    dists_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    min_dist = np.min(dists_from_current)

    # Dynamic tolerance: expand if many candidates are very close
    abs_tol = max(alpha * min_dist, beta)
    near_mask = np.abs(dists_from_current - min_dist) <= abs_tol
    near_candidates = unvisited_nodes[near_mask]

    # If only one candidate, return it immediately
    if len(near_candidates) == 1:
        return near_candidates[0]

    # Step 2: Capacity-protected regret tie-breaking
    # Additional protection: if a candidate's demand leaves little slack, boost its regret slightly
    slack_factor = 0.05  # fraction of rest_capacity considered tight
    tight_threshold = slack_factor * rest_capacity

    best_score = -np.inf
    chosen = near_candidates[0]

    for cand in near_candidates:
        # Base regret as postponement penalty: distance from depot minus direct insertion cost
        direct_cost = distance_matrix[current_node, cand]
        depot_to_cand = distance_matrix[depot, cand]
        regret_base = depot_to_cand - direct_cost

        # Capacity-protection bonus: slightly favor candidates whose demand does NOT consume most of the slack
        # This avoids picking a customer that leaves almost no room unless its regret is very high.
        demand_penalty = max(0.0, demands[cand] - (rest_capacity - tight_threshold))
        capacity_bonus = -demand_penalty  # negative penalty becomes positive preference for low-demand nodes

        # Combined score: regret plus scaled capacity bonus
        score = regret_base + 0.01 * capacity_bonus

        if score > best_score:
            best_score = score
            chosen = cand

    # Early return to depot only if explicitly requested by the caller via empty unvisited_nodes
    # In normal operation we always return a feasible customer here.
    return chosen

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        rest_capacity:   remaining vehicle capacity
        demands:         demand of every node (index 0 = depot demand = 0)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot

    n = len(unvisited_nodes)
    scores = np.zeros(n)

    for idx, cand in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
        # Immediate cost: distance from current to candidate plus return to depot estimate
        direct_cost = distance_matrix[current_node][cand]

        # Regret computation: difference between best and second-best insertion among other unvisited
        remaining = unvisited_nodes[unvisited_nodes != cand]
        if len(remaining) == 0:
            regret = 0.0
        else:
            # For each remaining node, compute best insertion after choosing 'cand'
            # Best insertion = min over possible positions in future? Simplified: 
            # We approximate by comparing insertion into the current partial route 
            # vs insertion later after depot return.
            # Instead, we compute for each other node the increase if inserted after cand vs before cand?
            # Simpler: compute for each other node its two smallest distances to any position in current+futue route?
            # We'll use a proxy: best and second-best distances from 'remaining' to the set {current, cand}
            # But true regret requires route context; we use a myopic regret based on distance to candidate vs depot.
            # Actually, we compute regret for each other node j: how much worse is inserting j later (after depot)
            # compared to inserting it now if possible? Since capacities vary, we ignore exact feasibility.
            # Here we define regret_i = for each other node k, find two smallest insertion costs (now/later) and take diff.
            # Approximation: regret_i = max_{k in remaining} (dist(cand,k) - min(dist(current,k), dist(depot,k))) clipped positive.
            # But simpler classic regret-k: cost increase if this candidate is taken now and another must be served later.
            # We'll compute for each other node: best possible insertion cost if candidate not chosen (min dist to any visited/unvisited?)
            # Too heavy; we'll use a simplified regret: for each other node, its distance to candidate minus distance to depot.
            # Then aggregate.
            pass

        # Simplified regret-like measure without full lookahead simulation:
        # Compute for each other node the penalty if this candidate consumes capacity.
        # We'll use a regret-2 style: for each other node, best insertion position is either directly from current,
        # or via depot (i.e., in next route). The "regret" is how much extra distance incurred if forced into next route.
        # But since we don't know future routes, we compare: if we go to candidate then later to other,
        # vs going to other directly now. Difference is (dist(current,cand)+dist(cand,other)) - dist(current,other).
        # However, capacity may prevent taking both now anyway.
        # So we compute an average "future inconvenience" caused by picking candidate.
        
        # Let's compute for each other node the minimal additional cost induced by choosing candidate first.
        # Future cost approximation: if we pick candidate now, other nodes will be served later possibly from depot.
        # Their access cost becomes dist(depot, other). If we could serve them immediately from current, cost is dist(current,other).
        # Increase = dist(depot,other) - dist(current,other). But that's independent of candidate.
        # Better: increase = dist(candidate,other) + dist(other,depot) - (direct from current?) too complex.
        
        # Practical fast heuristic: 
        # Score = alpha * direct_cost + beta * (sum over others of dist(candidate,other))/len(others)
        # where second term estimates clustering benefit.
        
        # Instead, implement a proper regret-1 (myopic) then extend to regret-n approximately.
        # Regret-1: choose candidate that maximizes the increase in cost for the second-best choice.
        # That is: for each candidate i, let best_other = argmin_{j!=i} dist(current,j). Let cost_if_not_chosen = dist(current,best_other).
        # The regret of not choosing i is zero for i itself? Wait, standard regret: for each customer, compute cost increase if it's not served now.
        # We'll do: for each candidate i, compute insertion cost c_i = dist(current,i).
        # Sort candidates by c_i ascending. Let best = min c_i, second_best = second min c_i.
        # Regret for i = (second_best - best) if i==best else (some value)? Not right.
        
        # Actually classic regret heuristic: for each unassigned customer, compute the cost difference between its best and second-best insertion position (in existing route). Then select customer with largest such difference.
        # In our sequential build, "insertion positions" are limited: insert after current node in same trip, or leave for next trip (cost approximated by distance from depot).
        # So for each candidate i, compute:
        # best_insertion_cost = min( dist(current,i),  # insert now in current trip
        #                            dist(depot,i) )   # leave for next trip (from depot start)
        # Actually, leaving for next trip means starting from depot later, so cost is dist(depot,i) but that ignores current position's influence.
        # More accurately: if not taken now, it will be served later from depot, so extra cost = dist(depot,i) - something? We'll ignore.
        # For simplicity, we define:
        # cost_now = dist(current,i)
        # cost_later = dist(depot,i)   # since next trip starts at depot
        # best = min(cost_now, cost_later)
        # second_best = max(cost_now, cost_later)
        # regret_i = second_best - best
        # But cost_now always <= cost_later usually? Not necessarily if depot is closer than current.
        # Anyway, regret_i = |cost_now - cost_later|.
        
        # We'll use a different approach: maximize marginal gain in serving now vs later.
        # Let's define for each candidate i:
        # gain_i = dist(depot,i) - dist(current,i)   # saving by serving now rather than from depot later
        # normalized_gain = gain_i / dist(depot,i) if dist(depot,i)>0 else 0
        # Then score_i = direct_cost * (1 - gamma*normalized_gain) to favor high-gain nodes even if slightly farther.
        
        # However, the problem asks for a new algorithm different from given.
        # Given algorithm used remoteness (average distance to other unvisited). Ours uses regret.
        # Implement regret-2 style without full lookahead simulation:
        # For each candidate i, consider two alternative scenarios: 
        # Scenario A: choose i now, then later choose the best other j from i.
        # Scenario B: choose the best other j now, then later choose i from j.
        # Compare total incremental distance.
        # We want to pick i that makes scenario A much better than scenario B.
        # Define for each i: 
        # best_pair = min_{j in remaining} [dist(current,i)+dist(i,j)]  # best continuation after i
        # alt_pair = min_{j in remaining, j!=best_j} [dist(current,j)+dist(j,i)]  # best alternative order
        # regret_i = alt_pair - best_pair  # negative if i is good? We want to minimize.
        
        # But computing all pairs is O(n^2). Acceptable for moderate n.
    
    # Re-implementing cleanly below:

    # Precompute distances from current to all unvisited
    dists_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]
    # Distances from depot to all unvisited
    dists_from_depot = distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]

    # For each candidate, compute its own direct cost
    # Also compute regret as difference between best and second-best insertion opportunities for other nodes
    # We'll approximate regret by considering the impact on the closest other node.

    regrets = np.zeros(n)
    for i in range(n):
        cand = unvisited_nodes[i]
        # Remaining nodes excluding i
        mask = np.ones(n, dtype=bool)
        mask[i] = False
        rem_nodes = unvisited_nodes[mask]
        if len(rem_nodes) == 0:
            regrets[i] = 0.0
            continue
        
        # For each other node, compute best insertion cost if we were to insert it after candidate
        # versus inserting it now directly from current (if capacity allowed, but ignored here).
        # Since capacity already filtered, we just look at geometric regret.
        # For each other node k, the cost increase if we postpone k to next route:
        # postponement_penalty_k = dist(depot,k) - min(dist(current,k), dist(candidate,k)+dist(candidate,depot)-dist(current,depot)?)
        # Too messy. Use simple regret definition:
        # For each other node k, best insertion position is either from current or from candidate.
        # Compute best = min(dist(current,k), dist(cand,k))
        # second_best = max(dist(current,k), dist(cand,k))
        # regret_for_k = second_best - best
        # Then regrets[i] = np.sum(regret_for_k) / len(rem_nodes)  # average regret imposed on others
        
        # Vectorized for speed:
        d_curr_to_others = distance_matrix[current_node][rem_nodes]
        d_cand_to_others = distance_matrix[cand][rem_nodes]
        best_two = np.minimum(d_curr_to_others, d_cand_to_others)
        worst_two = np.maximum(d_curr_to_others, d_cand_to_others)
        regret_others = worst_two - best_two
        regrets[i] = np.mean(regret_others)

    # Combine direct cost and regret
    # Normalize
    max_dist = np.max(dists_from_current) if np.max(dists_from_current) > 0 else 1
    max_regret = np.max(regrets) if np.max(regrets) > 0 else 1
    norm_dist = dists_from_current / max_dist
    norm_regret = regrets / max_regret

    # Weight: balance immediate distance and future flexibility
    # Higher regret means picking this candidate hurts others less? Wait, our regret_others measures how much worse off others are if we choose cand.
    # Larger regret_others means others suffer more if we choose cand, so we should avoid.
    # Thus score = distance + lambda*regret, minimize.
    weights = {'distance': 0.6, 'regret': 0.4}
    scores = weights['distance'] * norm_dist + weights['regret'] * norm_regret

    # Choose candidate with minimum score
    best_idx = np.argmin(scores)
    best_candidate = unvisited_nodes[best_idx]

    # Optional early return to depot: if best_candidate's direct cost is very large relative to others,
    # maybe it's better to restart. Check if returning now saves more than continuing.
    # Simple rule: if dists_from_current.min() > threshold * mean_distance, return depot.
    # We'll skip early depot return unless no feasible, which caller handles.

    return best_candidate

    return best_candidate

        # regret_i = alt_pair - best_pair  # negative if i is good? We want to minimize.
        
        # But computing all pairs is O(n^2). Acceptable for moderate n.
    
    # Re-implementing cleanly below:

    # Precompute distances from current to all unvisited
    dists_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]
    # Distances from depot to all unvisited
    dists_from_depot = distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]

    # For each candidate, compute its own direct cost
    # Also compute regret as difference between best and second-best insertion opportunities for other nodes
    # We'll approximate regret by considering the impact on the closest other node.

    regrets = np.zeros(n)
    for i in range(n):
        cand = unvisited_nodes[i]
        # Remaining nodes excluding i
        mask = np.ones(n, dtype=bool)
        mask[i] = False
        rem_nodes = unvisited_nodes[mask]
        if len(rem_nodes) == 0:
            regrets[i] = 0.0
            continue
        
        # For each other node, compute best insertion cost if we were to insert it after candidate
        # versus inserting it now directly from current (if capacity allowed, but ignored here).
        # Since capacity already filtered, we just look at geometric regret.
        # For each other node k, the cost increase if we postpone k to next route:
        # postponement_penalty_k = dist(depot,k) - min(dist(current,k), dist(candidate,k)+dist(candidate,depot)-dist(current,depot)?)
        # Too messy. Use simple regret definition:
        # For each other node k, best insertion position is either from current or from candidate.
        # Compute best = min(dist(current,k), dist(cand,k))
        # second_best = max(dist(current,k), dist(cand,k))
        # regret_for_k = second_best - best
        # Then regrets[i] = np.sum(regret_for_k) / len(rem_nodes)  # average regret imposed on others
        
        # Vectorized for speed:
        d_curr_to_others = distance_matrix[current_node][rem_nodes]
        d_cand_to_others = distance_matrix[cand][rem_nodes]
        best_two = np.minimum(d_curr_to_others, d_cand_to_others)
        worst_two = np.maximum(d_curr_to_others, d_cand_to_others)
        regret_others = worst_two - best_two
        regrets[i] = np.mean(regret_others)

    # Combine direct cost and regret
    # Normalize
    max_dist = np.max(dists_from_current) if np.max(dists_from_current) > 0 else 1
    max_regret = np.max(regrets) if np.max(regrets) > 0 else 1
    norm_dist = dists_from_current / max_dist
    norm_regret = regrets / max_regret

    # Weight: balance immediate distance and future flexibility
    # Higher regret means picking this candidate hurts others less? Wait, our regret_others measures how much worse off others are if we choose cand.
    # Larger regret_others means others suffer more if we choose cand, so we should avoid.
    # Thus score = distance + lambda*regret, minimize.
    weights = {'distance': 0.6, 'regret': 0.4}
    scores = weights['distance'] * norm_dist + weights['regret'] * norm_regret

    # Choose candidate with minimum score
    best_idx = np.argmin(scores)
    best_candidate = unvisited_nodes[best_idx]

    # Optional early return to depot: if best_candidate's direct cost is very large relative to others,
    # maybe it's better to restart. Check if returning now saves more than continuing.
    # Simple rule: if dists_from_current.min() > threshold * mean_distance, return depot.
    # We'll skip early depot return unless no feasible, which caller handles.

    return best_candidate

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot

    # Force parameters (tuned empirically)
    alpha = 1.0      # distance pull coefficient
    beta = 0.8       # demand push coefficient
    gamma = 0.4      # angular repulsion coefficient

    curr_pos = current_node
    pos = unvisited_nodes

    # --- Distance Pull (inverse square law style) ---
    dists = distance_matrix[curr_pos, pos].astype(float)
    # Avoid division by zero
    eps = 1e-10
    inv_dist_sq = 1.0 / (dists ** 2 + eps)
    pull_force = alpha * inv_dist_sq

    # --- Demand Push (penalize large demands if capacity is tight) ---
    utilizations = demands[pos] / rest_capacity
    # Push grows superlinearly with utilization
    push_force = beta * (utilizations ** 2)

    # --- Angular Repulsion (encourage covering diverse directions) ---
    # Get vector from current node to depot for reference direction
    # Compute angles of candidates relative to depot direction
    ref_vec = distance_matrix[curr_pos, depot]  # scalar distance, not vector; we approximate angle via depot proximity
    # Simpler: use angular spread penalty based on deviation from mean angular position of remaining nodes
    # Approximate with cosine similarity using distances to depot and current
    # cos(theta) ~ (d_cd^2 + d_cc^2 - d_dd^2)/(2*d_cd*d_cc) but we simplify:
    # Use depot distance as proxy for direction; nodes far from depot in similar directions cluster.
    # Instead, compute penalty for being too aligned with the average direction of all candidates.
    # Heuristic: penalize nodes whose depot distance is too similar to others? Skip complex trig.
    # Alternative: reward nodes that lie away from the line current-depot to promote route expansion.
    # We use: repel = exp(-|dist_to_depot_diff|) where diff is relative to median depot distance.
    dep_dists = distance_matrix[pos, depot]
    median_dep_dist = np.median(dep_dists)
    dir_penalty = np.exp(-np.abs(dep_dists - median_dep_dist))  # similar depot distance -> similar direction -> penalty
    # Normalize
    repel_force = gamma * dir_penalty

    # Net attractive force: higher is better (pull minus penalties)
    net_force = pull_force - push_force - repel_force

    best_idx = np.argmax(net_force)
    return unvisited_nodes[best_idx]

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction.

    Args:
        current_node:    index of the current node (0 = depot)
        depot:           index of the depot (always 0)
        unvisited_nodes: array of feasible unvisited customer indices
                         (already filtered to satisfy remaining capacity)
        rest_capacity:   remaining vehicle capacity
        demands:         demand of every node (index 0 = depot demand = 0)
        distance_matrix: pairwise Euclidean distance matrix
    Returns:
        Index of the next node to visit, or 0 to return to the depot early.
    """
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute distances from current node to all feasible unvisited nodes
    dist_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Find the nearest node (fallback candidate)
    nearest_idx = np.argmin(dist_from_current)
    nearest_node = unvisited_nodes[nearest_idx]
    min_distance = dist_from_current[nearest_idx]
    
    # If only one feasible node, return it immediately
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 1:
        return nearest_node
    
    # Calculate regret values for each feasible node
    regrets = np.zeros(len(unvisited_nodes))
    for i, node in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
        # Distance from current to this node
        d_current = distance_matrix[current_node, node]
        
        # For regret calculation, consider insertion after current position
        # Regret = cost difference between inserting now vs. in future
        
        # Create temporary list without current candidate
        other_nodes = np.concatenate([unvisited_nodes[:i], unvisited_nodes[i+1:]])
        
        if len(other_nodes) > 0:
            # Best alternative insertion point for this node would be from its nearest neighbor among others
            # Compute minimum distance from this node to any other feasible node
            dist_to_others = distance_matrix[node, other_nodes]
            min_alt_distance = np.min(dist_to_others)
            
            # Regret approximation: difference between being served now vs. worst-case future detour
            # Here we use a simplified regret: difference between nearest and second-nearest from current
            sorted_distances = np.sort(distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes])
            if len(sorted_distances) >= 2:
                best = sorted_distances[0]
                second_best = sorted_distances[1] if len(sorted_distances) > 1 else sorted_distances[0]
                regret_val = second_best - best
                
                # Map regret to individual nodes
                if abs(d_current - best) < 1e-10:
                    regrets[i] = max(0, regret_val)
                else:
                    # For non-nearest nodes, estimate potential regret if postponed
                    regrets[i] = max(0, d_current - best)
            else:
                regrets[i] = 0
        else:
            regrets[i] = 0
    
    # Normalize scores
    norm_factor_dist = min_distance + 1e-6  # Avoid division by zero
    normalized_distances = dist_from_current / norm_factor_dist
    
    norm_factor_regret = np.max(regrets) + 1e-6
    normalized_regrets = regrets / norm_factor_regret if norm_factor_regret > 0 else np.ones_like(regrets)
    
    # Combined score: balance proximity and regret
    # Weight can be adjusted; here using equal weighting
    alpha = 0.7  # Weight for distance (closer is better)
    beta = 0.3   # Weight for regret (higher regret is better)
    
    combined_scores = alpha * (1 - normalized_distances) + beta * normalized_regrets
    
    # Select node with highest combined score
    selected_idx = np.argmax(combined_scores)
    selected_node = unvisited_nodes[selected_idx]
    
    return selected_node

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction."""
    # If no feasible customers remain, return to depot
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot

    # Compute distances from current_node to all unvisited customers
    dist_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]

    # Strategy decision point: at depot or very start of route?
    # If current_node is depot, consider far-first seeding
    if current_node == depot:
        # Distance from depot to each unvisited customer
        dist_from_depot = distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]
        # Choose the farthest feasible customer
        farthest_idx = np.argmax(dist_from_depot)
        return unvisited_nodes[farthest_idx]

    # Regret insertion logic for non-depot current node
    # For each unvisited node u, find its two smallest insertion costs
    # Here insertion cost is simply direct distance from current_node to u
    # because we are building sequentially (not inserting into an existing sequence).
    # However, regret can still be computed relative to alternative "next" positions.
    # We approximate regret as: how much worse is the second-best choice compared to the best?
    # Since we are picking ONE next node, we compute regret per candidate:
    # For each candidate u, imagine NOT choosing u now; what would be the extra cost later?
    # Simple proxy: among all other unvisited nodes v != u, compute min distance from current_node to v.
    # Then regret_u = (best distance among others) - distance_to_u, but this needs careful handling.
    # A more stable regret for sequential construction:
    #   regret = distance_from_current[u] - min_distance_among_all_unvisited
    # But that just reorders by distance. True regret should look ahead to future cost increase.
    # Following strategy card: compute best and second-best distance/detour from current_node.
    # Since we are going directly from current to next, detour = distance(current, u).
    # For second-best, we need the next-best alternative destination from current_node.
    # So: sort distances from current_node to unvisited nodes, take first and second.
    sorted_indices_by_dist = np.argsort(dist_from_current)
    # If only one candidate, fallback to nearest (which is that one)
    if len(sorted_indices_by_dist) == 1:
        return unvisited_nodes[sorted_indices_by_dist[0]]

    # Best and second-best distances
    best_idx = sorted_indices_by_dist[0]
    second_best_idx = sorted_indices_by_dist[1]
    best_dist = dist_from_current[best_idx]
    second_best_dist = dist_from_current[second_best_idx]

    # Compute regret for each candidate as difference between its distance and best distance,
    # but weighted by how much farther it is than the best alternative? Actually card says:
    # "regret = second_best - best" for each candidate? That's ambiguous.
    # Standard regret: for each customer, compute insertion cost in current position vs elsewhere.
    # In sequential greedy, simpler regret measure:
    #   regret(u) = (distance to u) - (min distance among all unvisited)
    # But that's zero for the nearest. Not useful.
    # Better: for each u, consider the penalty if u is NOT chosen now.
    # Approximate by: distance from current to u minus distance from depot to u? No.
    # We'll follow a hybrid: compute for each u the difference between:
    #   1) cost if we go to u now (distance(current,u))
    #   2) cost if we postpone u to next route (distance(depot,u) + distance(u,?)) unknown.
    # Instead, we use a practical implementation from literature:
    #   regret_k(u) = sum_{i=1..k} (cost_i(u) - best_cost_i) where cost_i(u) is i-th best insertion place.
    # Here k=2, and insertion places are limited to "now" vs "later", but later is undefined.
    # Given the API constraint (deterministic, bounded), we adapt:
    #   Step 1: Identify nearest feasible customer (best candidate).
    #   Step 2: For each other customer, compute how much longer the trip would be if we skip it now
    #           and have to come back later from somewhere else. Proxy: distance from current to u
    #           versus distance from depot to u. The difference (dist(curr,u)-dist(depot,u)) indicates
    #           savings if served now. Higher saving means higher regret if postponed.
    # But this may favor far customers incorrectly.
    # Simpler effective heuristic from retrieved strategies: combine ideas.
    # We'll do:
    #   If there exists a customer with demand > half of remaining capacity, prioritize it
    #   to avoid leaving small scraps? Not in cards.
    # Final decision: use a scoring that blends:
    #   - Distance from current (nearest preferred normally)
    #   - Far-from-depot priority early in route (but we already handled at depot)
    #   - Regret: penalize skipping a customer that is far from depot but close to us now.
    # Score = (distance from depot to u) / (distance from current to u + epsilon)
    # High ratio means far from depot (good to serve now) and close to current (low immediate cost).

    # Hybrid rule:
    # Compute scores for all unvisited nodes
    dist_from_depot_to_u = distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]
    eps = 1e-10
    scores = dist_from_depot_to_u / (dist_from_current + eps)

    # Check if we are still "early" in route (rest_capacity close to initial? unknown initial).
    # Without knowing initial capacity, we use a simple threshold: if rest_capacity is high (>50% max?)
    # but we don't have max capacity. So instead, trigger far-first logic only at depot (done above).
    # Now apply regret-style tie-breaking:
    # Among top candidates by score, pick the one with highest regret.
    # To compute regret for each u: suppose we don't pick u now, the best alternative is the nearest other node.
    # The extra cost incurred by postponing u is approximated by: 
    #   regret(u) = distance(current, u) - min(distance(current, v) for v != u)
    #              + alpha * (distance(depot, u) - min(distance(depot, v) for v != u))
    # But we simplify: use the standard "insertion regret" for two alternatives:
    #   Alternative 1: insert u right after current (cost = distance(current,u))
    #   Alternative 2: insert u later after some other node w (cost ≈ distance(w,u) - distance(w,?)) unknown.
    # Practical approach from many implementations: regret_2(u) = 
    #   [cost_second_best_position(u) - cost_best_position(u)]
    # where best_position is now (current->u), second best is from depot later (depot->u).
    # So regret = (distance(depot,u) - distance(current,u))? That could be negative.
    # Actually, if distance(current,u) < distance(depot,u), serving now saves (depot-u)-(curr-u) = depot-curr? constant.
    # Let's directly implement the strategy card's literal suggestion with available info:
    # "compute best and second-best distance/detour from current_node"
    # We interpret: for each candidate u, the "detour" is distance(current,u).
    # The "best" and "second-best" refer to among all candidates, not per candidate.
    # Then "regret = second_best - best" is a single number, not per candidate. That's odd.
    # Re-reading: "prefer customers with high detour regret" implies per-customer regret.
    # So: for each customer u, compute its best possible insertion place (here only "after current")
    # and its second-best insertion place (after some other customer v in unvisited).
    # Since we don't know future route, we approximate second-best as: min over v in unvisited, v!=u of distance(v,u).
    # Then regret(u) = min_v≠u distance(v,u) - distance(current,u).
    # Positive regret means it's cheaper to serve now than to pair with another customer later.
    # Implementation:

    # Precompute minimal distance between u and any other unvisited customer
    min_dist_other = np.full(len(unvisited_nodes), np.inf)
    for i, u in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
        mask = np.ones(len(unvisited_nodes), dtype=bool)
        mask[i] = False
        others = unvisited_nodes[mask]
        if len(others) > 0:
            min_dist_other[i] = np.min(distance_matrix[u][others])
        else:
            min_dist_other[i] = 0  # no other, regret irrelevant

    regret_vals = min_dist_other - dist_from_current

    # Combine: primary criterion = regret, secondary = closeness
    # Choose node with maximum regret; break ties by minimum distance from current.
    max_regret = np.max(regret_vals)
    # Candidates with max regret
    candidates = np.where(np.isclose(regret_vals, max_regret))[0]
    if len(candidates) == 1:
        selected_idx = candidates[0]
    else:
        # Tie-break by nearest among them
        selected_idx = candidates[np.argmin(dist_from_current[candidates])]

    # Fallback safety: ensure we never exceed capacity (should be guaranteed by input filter)
    # Also avoid premature depot return unless necessary.
    return unvisited_nodes[selected_idx]

    # Combine: primary criterion = regret, secondary = closeness
    # Choose node with maximum regret; break ties by minimum distance from current.
    max_regret = np.max(regret_vals)
    # Candidates with max regret
    candidates = np.where(np.isclose(regret_vals, max_regret))[0]
    if len(candidates) == 1:
        selected_idx = candidates[0]
    else:
        # Tie-break by nearest among them
        selected_idx = candidates[np.argmin(dist_from_current[candidates])]

    # Fallback safety: ensure we never exceed capacity (should be guaranteed by input filter)
    # Also avoid premature depot return unless necessary.
    return unvisited_nodes[selected_idx]

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    """Select the next node to visit in a CVRP greedy construction."""
    # If no feasible customers remain, return to depot
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot

    # Compute distances from current_node to all unvisited customers
    dist_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]

    # Strategy decision point: at depot or very start of route?
    # If current_node is depot, consider far-first seeding
    if current_node == depot:
        # Distance from depot to each unvisited customer
        dist_from_depot = distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]
        # Choose the farthest feasible customer
        farthest_idx = np.argmax(dist_from_depot)
        return unvisited_nodes[farthest_idx]

    # Regret insertion logic for non-depot current node
    # For each unvisited node u, find its two smallest insertion costs
    # Here insertion cost is simply direct distance from current_node to u
    # because we are building sequentially (not inserting into an existing sequence).
    # However, regret can still be computed relative to alternative "next" positions.
    # We approximate regret as: how much worse is the second-best choice compared to the best?
    # Since we are picking ONE next node, we compute regret per candidate:
    # For each candidate u, imagine NOT choosing u now; what would be the extra cost later?
    # Simple proxy: among all other unvisited nodes v != u, compute min distance from current_node to v.
    # Then regret_u = (best distance among others) - distance_to_u, but this needs careful handling.
    # A more stable regret for sequential construction:
    #   regret = distance_from_current[u] - min_distance_among_all_unvisited
    # But that just reorders by distance. True regret should look ahead to future cost increase.
    # Following strategy card: compute best and second-best distance/detour from current_node.
    # Since we are going directly from current to next, detour = distance(current, u).
    # For second-best, we need the next-best alternative destination from current_node.
    # So: sort distances from current_node to unvisited nodes, take first and second.
    sorted_indices_by_dist = np.argsort(dist_from_current)
    # If only one candidate, fallback to nearest (which is that one)
    if len(sorted_indices_by_dist) == 1:
        return unvisited_nodes[sorted_indices_by_dist[0]]

    # Best and second-best distances
    best_idx = sorted_indices_by_dist[0]
    second_best_idx = sorted_indices_by_dist[1]
    best_dist = dist_from_current[best_idx]
    second_best_dist = dist_from_current[second_best_idx]

    # Compute regret for each candidate as difference between its distance and best distance,
    # but weighted by how much farther it is than the best alternative? Actually card says:
    # "regret = second_best - best" for each candidate? That's ambiguous.
    # Standard regret: for each customer, compute insertion cost in current position vs elsewhere.
    # In sequential greedy, simpler regret measure:
    #   regret(u) = (distance to u) - (min distance among all unvisited)
    # But that's zero for the nearest. Not useful.
    # Better: for each u, consider the penalty if u is NOT chosen now.
    # Approximate by: distance from current to u minus distance from depot to u? No.
    # We'll follow a hybrid: compute for each u the difference between:
    #   1) cost if we go to u now (distance(current,u))
    #   2) cost if we postpone u to next route (distance(depot,u) + distance(u,?)) unknown.
    # Instead, we use a practical implementation from literature:
    #   regret_k(u) = sum_{i=1..k} (cost_i(u) - best_cost_i) where cost_i(u) is i-th best insertion place.
    # Here k=2, and insertion places are limited to "now" vs "later", but later is undefined.
    # Given the API constraint (deterministic, bounded), we adapt:
    #   Step 1: Identify nearest feasible customer (best candidate).
    #   Step 2: For each other customer, compute how much longer the trip would be if we skip it now
    #           and have to come back later from somewhere else. Proxy: distance from current to u
    #           versus distance from depot to u. The difference (dist(curr,u)-dist(depot,u)) indicates
    #           savings if served now. Higher saving means higher regret if postponed.
    # But this may favor far customers incorrectly.
    # Simpler effective heuristic from retrieved strategies: combine ideas.
    # We'll do:
    #   If there exists a customer with demand > half of remaining capacity, prioritize it
    #   to avoid leaving small scraps? Not in cards.
    # Final decision: use a scoring that blends:
    #   - Distance from current (nearest preferred normally)
    #   - Far-from-depot priority early in route (but we already handled at depot)
    #   - Regret: penalize skipping a customer that is far from depot but close to us now.
    # Score = (distance from depot to u) / (distance from current to u + epsilon)
    # High ratio means far from depot (good to serve now) and close to current (low immediate cost).

    # Hybrid rule:
    # Compute scores for all unvisited nodes
    dist_from_depot_to_u = distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]
    eps = 1e-10
    scores = dist_from_depot_to_u / (dist_from_current + eps)

    # Check if we are still "early" in route (rest_capacity close to initial? unknown initial).
    # Without knowing initial capacity, we use a simple threshold: if rest_capacity is high (>50% max?)
    # but we don't have max capacity. So instead, trigger far-first logic only at depot (done above).
    # Now apply regret-style tie-breaking:
    # Among top candidates by score, pick the one with highest regret.
    # To compute regret for each u: suppose we don't pick u now, the best alternative is the nearest other node.
    # The extra cost incurred by postponing u is approximated by: 
    #   regret(u) = distance(current, u) - min(distance(current, v) for v != u)
    #              + alpha * (distance(depot, u) - min(distance(depot, v) for v != u))
    # But we simplify: use the standard "insertion regret" for two alternatives:
    #   Alternative 1: insert u right after current (cost = distance(current,u))
    #   Alternative 2: insert u later after some other node w (cost ≈ distance(w,u) - distance(w,?)) unknown.
    # Practical approach from many implementations: regret_2(u) = 
    #   [cost_second_best_position(u) - cost_best_position(u)]
    # where best_position is now (current->u), second best is from depot later (depot->u).
    # So regret = (distance(depot,u) - distance(current,u))? That could be negative.
    # Actually, if distance(current,u) < distance(depot,u), serving now saves (depot-u)-(curr-u) = depot-curr? constant.
    # Let's directly implement the strategy card's literal suggestion with available info:
    # "compute best and second-best distance/detour from current_node"
    # We interpret: for each candidate u, the "detour" is distance(current,u).
    # The "best" and "second-best" refer to among all candidates, not per candidate.
    # Then "regret = second_best - best" is a single number, not per candidate. That's odd.
    # Re-reading: "prefer customers with high detour regret" implies per-customer regret.
    # So: for each customer u, compute its best possible insertion place (here only "after current")
    # and its second-best insertion place (after some other customer v in unvisited).
    # Since we don't know future route, we approximate second-best as: min over v in unvisited, v!=u of distance(v,u).
    # Then regret(u) = min_v≠u distance(v,u) - distance(current,u).
    # Positive regret means it's cheaper to serve now than to pair with another customer later.
    # Implementation:

    # Precompute minimal distance between u and any other unvisited customer
    min_dist_other = np.full(len(unvisited_nodes), np.inf)
    for i, u in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
        mask = np.ones(len(unvisited_nodes), dtype=bool)
        mask[i] = False
        others = unvisited_nodes[mask]
        if len(others) > 0:
            min_dist_other[i] = np.min(distance_matrix[u][others])
        else:
            min_dist_other[i] = 0  # no other, regret irrelevant

    regret_vals = min_dist_other - dist_from_current

    # Combine: primary criterion = regret, secondary = closeness
    # Choose node with maximum regret; break ties by minimum distance from current.
    max_regret = np.max(regret_vals)
    # Candidates with max regret
    candidates = np.where(np.isclose(regret_vals, max_regret))[0]
    if len(candidates) == 1:
        selected_idx = candidates[0]
    else:
        # Tie-break by nearest among them
        selected_idx = candidates[np.argmin(dist_from_current[candidates])]

    # Fallback safety: ensure we never exceed capacity (should be guaranteed by input filter)
    # Also avoid premature depot return unless necessary.
    return unvisited_nodes[selected_idx]

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot

    if current_node == depot:
        distances_from_depot = distance_matrix[depot][unvisited_nodes]
        # Prefer farthest from depot, but also consider demand size relative to rest_capacity
        demand_ratios = demands[unvisited_nodes] / rest_capacity
        scores = distances_from_depot * (1 + demand_ratios)
        best_idx = np.argmax(scores)
        return unvisited_nodes[best_idx]

    n = len(unvisited_nodes)
    regret_scores = np.zeros(n)
    for i, candidate in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
        dist_to_candidate = distance_matrix[current_node][candidate]
        other_dists = [distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes[j]] for j in range(n) if j != i]
        second_best = min(other_dists) if other_dists else dist_to_candidate
        regret = second_best - dist_to_candidate
        capacity_penalty = demands[candidate] / rest_capacity
        regret_scores[i] = regret - 0.5 * capacity_penalty * distance_matrix[depot][candidate]

    best_idx = np.argmax(regret_scores)
    return unvisited_nodes[best_idx]

import numpy as np

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    n_candidates = len(unvisited_nodes)
    distances_from_current = distance_matrix[current_node, unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Step 1: For each candidate, find two cheapest ways to continue after serving it
    regrets = np.zeros(n_candidates)
    for i, cand in enumerate(unvisited_nodes):
        cand_demand = demands[cand]
        new_capacity = rest_capacity - cand_demand
        
        # Remaining feasible customers after choosing cand
        remaining_mask = (demands[unvisited_nodes] <= new_capacity) & (np.arange(len(unvisited_nodes)) != i)
        remaining_cust = unvisited_nodes[remaining_mask]
        
        # Option 1: go directly to depot after cand
        opt1 = distance_matrix[cand, depot]
        
        # Option 2: go to nearest feasible remaining customer after cand
        if len(remaining_cust) > 0:
            dists_from_cand = distance_matrix[cand, remaining_cust]
            opt2 = np.min(dists_from_cand)
        else:
            opt2 = distance_matrix[cand, depot]
        
        # Regret: how much worse is the second-best continuation compared to the best?
        # Here we define regret as (opt2 - opt1) because opt1 is always depot-return.
        # To encourage choices that keep options open, we want small positive or even negative regret.
        regret_val = opt2 - opt1
        regrets[i] = max(regret_val, 0)  # clip at zero to ignore beneficial depot-return cases
        
        # Scale regret by capacity utilization: higher utilization → smaller regret acceptable
        cap_util = cand_demand / rest_capacity
        regrets[i] *= (1.0 + 2.0 * cap_util)  # weight more heavily when capacity is tight
    
    # Normalize distances and regrets to [0,1]
    if np.max(distances_from_current) > np.min(distances_from_current):
        norm_dist = (distances_from_current - np.min(distances_from_current)) / \
                    (np.max(distances_from_current) - np.min(distances_from_current))
    else:
        norm_dist = np.ones_like(distances_from_current)
    
    if np.max(regrets) > np.min(regrets):
        norm_regret = (regrets - np.min(regrets)) / (np.max(regrets) - np.min(regrets))
    else:
        norm_regret = np.zeros_like(regrets)
    
    # Combined score: trade-off between proximity and future flexibility
    alpha = 0.7  # weight on distance vs regret
    scores = alpha * norm_dist + (1 - alpha) * norm_regret
    
    # Select candidate with minimum combined score
    chosen_idx = np.argmin(scores)
    chosen = unvisited_nodes[chosen_idx]
    
    # Early depot return if choosing this customer leaves no feasible successor AND
    # returning now instead of visiting chosen first saves distance
    new_cap = rest_capacity - demands[chosen]
    remaining_after = unvisited_nodes[(demands[unvisited_nodes] <= new_cap) &
                                      (unvisited_nodes != chosen)]
    if len(remaining_after) == 0 and len(unvisited_nodes) > 1:
        # Compare: current->depot->chosen vs current->chosen->depot
        cost_if_return_now = distance_matrix[current_node, depot] + distance_matrix[depot, chosen]
        cost_if_visit_first = distance_matrix[current_node, chosen] + distance_matrix[chosen, depot]
        if cost_if_return_now <= cost_if_visit_first + 1e-9:
            return depot
    
    return chosen

def select_next_node(current_node: int, depot: int, unvisited_nodes: np.ndarray,
                     rest_capacity: float, demands: np.ndarray,
                     distance_matrix: np.ndarray) -> int:
    if len(unvisited_nodes) == 0:
        return depot
    
    # Compute distances from current node to all unvisited nodes
    distances = distance_matrix[current_node][unvisited_nodes]
    
    # Find the smallest distance
    min_dist = np.min(distances)
    
    # Get all nodes within 10% of the smallest distance (close candidates)
    threshold = min_dist * 1.1
    close_mask = distances <= threshold
    close_nodes = unvisited_nodes[close_mask]
    
    # Among close nodes, select the one with largest demand
    close_demands = demands[close_nodes]
    best_idx = np.argmax(close_demands)
    return close_nodes[best_idx]
